title: MKT10009 Lecture 7 Part 1

content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 7 - Chapter 10 – “P” for Place: Customer value fulfilmentPart 1 - Supply chains and the value delivery network.
Chapter 10 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 7
10.1 Describe the nature of supply chain management and how marketing channels add value.
10.2 Describe the nature of marketing channels and how they organise to create value.
10.3 Discuss retailing, retailers’ marketing decisions and the different ways of classifying stores and describe retailing trends and developments.
10.4 Compare and contrast the different types of wholesalers and their marketing decisions and discuss wholesaling trends.

Part 1  - Supply chains and the value delivery network.

So, what is marketing? Customer-driven market strategy
Figure 1.6:
An expanded model of the marketing process

Supply chains and the value delivery network (1 of 12)
A supply chain is a system of efficiently and effectively producing, making and getting products to end-users.
A supply chain pertains to both physical goods and services.
Supply chain management is the process of managing upstream and downstream value-added flows of materials, final goods and related information among suppliers, the marketing organisation, resellers and final consumers.
Q: ‘Information systems play a critical role in managing supply chains.’ Do you agree with this statement? Why, or why not?

Figure 10.1
Supply chain management

Supply chains and the value delivery network (3 of 12)
Marketing logistics (physical distribution) are the tasks involved in planning, implementing and controlling the physical flow of materials, final goods and related information from points of origin to points of consumption in order to meet customer requirements at a profit.
Q: Some describe marketing logistics as ‘getting the right product to the right customer in the right place at the right time’. How accurate do you think this description is?

Supply chains and the value delivery network (4 of 12)
A value delivery network comprises the marketing organisation, suppliers, distributors and, ultimately, customers who partner with each other to improve the performance of the entire system.
Some typical supply chain decisions involve:
whether or not to sole-source operations
plant location and process
scheduling
whether to supply from production or inventory
the firm’s environmental sustainability efforts.
Q: Give some examples of supply chain decisions for (a) a car manufacturer and (b) a grocery chain.

Figure 10.2
Supply chain for a typical product

Supply chains and the value delivery network (6 of 12)
Supply chain goals and trade-offs
Supply chain trade-offs are the trade-offs in costs areas involved when deciding on the service level the organisation will offer customers.
Supply chains add value through decisions or activities such as:
cycle time reductions
conversion operations location
purchasing
manufacturing and operations process decisions.
Q: What are some other decisions or activities through which supply chains add value?

Supply chains and the value delivery network (7 of 12)
Major logistics functions
The main logistics functions include:
warehousing
inventory management
transportation
logistics information management.

Supply chains and the value delivery network (8 of 12)
Major logistics functions (cont.)
Warehousing
Key warehousing decisions include:
how many warehouses?
what types of warehouse?
where to locate warehouses?
Q: What is the difference between a warehouse and a distribution centre?

Supply chains and the value delivery network (9 of 12)
Major logistics functions (cont.)
Inventory management
In managing inventory, firms must balance the costs of carrying larger inventories against resulting sales and profits.
Techniques for improving efficiency and reducing costs include:
just-in-time (JIT) logistics networks
radio frequency identification (RFID) tags.
Q: ‘Inventory management affects customer satisfaction.’ How?

Supply chains and the value delivery network (10 of 12)
Major logistics functions (cont.)
Transportation
The choice of transportation carriers affects the pricing of products, delivery performance and condition of the goods when they arrive – all of which affect customer satisfaction.
Main modes of transport are:
trucks
railways
sea carriers
pipelines
air carriers
internet.

Supply chains and the value delivery network (11 of 12)
Transportation (cont.)
Shippers also use multimodal transport which involves combining two or more modes of transportation:
piggyback – combines rail and trucks
fishyback – combines water transport and trucks
trainship
airtruck
Q: What are some advantages and disadvantages of multimodal transport?

Supply chains and the value delivery network (12 of 12)
Major logistics functions (cont.)
Logistics information management
Companies need simple, accessible, fast and accurate processes for capturing, processing and sharing channel information.
The most widely used method for sharing and managing information is electronic data interchange (EDI), primarily via the internet.
Other methods include:
vendor-managed inventory (VMI) systems
continuous inventory replenishment systems.

Supply chains and the value delivery network
A: This is an accurate statement. Not only does effective marketing logistics increase customer service and satisfaction, but it will also reduce total product costs. This is because supply chain costs can make up to 30 - 40% of the total cost of a product.
A: Yes, information systems play a critical role because they provide data that enables well-informed decisions to be made on matters such as selecting sources of supply, sales forecasting, production and purchasing scheduling, and replenishment of inventory (stock).
Q: ‘Information systems play a critical role in managing supply chains.’ Do you agree with this statement? Why, or why not?
Q: Some describe marketing logistics as ‘getting the right product to the right customer in the right place at the right time’. How accurate do you think this description is?

A: Car manufacturer: Cross docking - cars can be loaded onto trucks direct from the factory or the ship and delivered direct to the dealer for pre-delivery preparation. By doing this the costs of passing the cars through central distribution centres is avoided. If the car was to go through central distribution centres they would have to be unloaded, and then re-loaded onto trucks to deliver to the dealers. Grocery chain: some goods can be delivered direct from the supplier to the stores, notably high-volume perishables such as milk and bread. This means that the product is delivered to the stores with a longer expiry date than if it were to go through the central distribution centre and then to the store. Therefore, product is more likely to be sold to consumers before reaching the expiry date. So cost are reduced (1) less need to throw-out expired stock, (2) there is no need to double-handle goods by going through the grocery chain’s own central distribution centre.
Q: Give some examples of supply chain decisions for (a) a car manufacturer and (b) a grocery chain.

Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) enables the exchange between supply chain participants of documents such as stock levels, orders, delivery information, invoices and payments. This makes the process much faster, more accurate, and much lower cost. Electronic scanning of sales at Point of Sale using Quick Retail Technologies (QRT) enables immediate update to the stock records and this in turn enables more accurate and fast stock replenishment to the retail outlets thereby eliminating out-of-stocks and lost sales at the retail level.
AND: Grocery chain: fruit and vegetables can be washed and packed into trays on the farm. These trays can then be delivered direct to the supermarket and immediately placed on the supermarket’s selling-floor without any need for double-handling through a central distribution centre or in the store.
Q: What are some other decisions or activities through which supply chains add value?

Supply chains and the value delivery network
A: Warehouses are places where products are kept in stock awaiting orders from customers. Distribution centres receive products and despatch them immediately or very soon after receipt.
Q: What is the difference between a warehouse and a distribution centre?

Supply chains and the value delivery network
A: Multimodal transport is where a single contract for delivery of the product is performed by at least two different modes of transport, such as a road > sea >road or road > rail > road. The carrier (the multimodal carrier) is legally responsible for the entire carriage of product from point-of-origin to point-of-delivery. The advantage to the client is that they need manage relationships with one entity only, usually called a “freight forwarder”. A disadvantage would be that the freight forwarder may or may not provide up-to-date information regarding the location of the product and likely delivery date.
Q: What are some advantages and disadvantages of multimodal transport?

Coming up next
Part 2 - The nature of marketing channels and value creation.
